documents_processed: 79102-00_BOM.pdf (cross-referenced only -- full line breakdown already captured in stadler_structured.json, not duplicated here)
not_provided: 79110-00_BOM.pdf (insulation, fresh-water tank -- mass 3.64 kg known only via 79100-00 parent reference)
79210-00_BOM.pdf (insulation, waste-water tank -- mass 4.87 kg known only via 79200-00 parent reference)
Inventor xlsx export (export issue on user's end, unresolved as of this analysis -- no Item-hierarchy cross-check or embedded thumbnails for this source, unlike the 80000 UWC fixture)

images.reason: No Inventor xlsx export was available for this source.
images.backfill_mechanism: Additive, no re-run required: upload to Supabase Storage bucket part-photos under STIRG/stadler-79000/<descriptive-or-sachnummer-name>.<ext> whenever a source becomes available, then cross-reference by sachnummer against stadler_bom_lines below. No schema or JSON-structure change needed to add images later.

findings_for_cross_analysis: Confirms and refines OQ-62(6): reuse between the SWC and UWC product lines is two-directional, not SWC-to-UWC only. 79101-09 (Flansch), 79101-10 (Schildbrücke), 79101-21 (Gewindehülse 1"), and 79101-24 (C-Schiene) are natively defined here in 79101-00 (SWC fresh-water shell) and are reused in 79201-00 (sibling SWC waste-water shell); 79101-09/-10/-21 are additionally the same part numbers already on record inside 80201-00 (UWC's waste-water shell), with matching qty and mass within normal rounding. 79201-32 (Stutzen 3/4"), 79201-36 and 79201-42 (Gewindeinsert) are natively defined in 79201-00 itself and are the ones previously found inside 80201-00. So both shells contribute native fittings to a shared catalog drawn on across sibling shells and across the SWC/UWC product line.

Weld-type-line counts match exactly between this source's welded shells and their UWC counterparts: 79101-00 has 10 weld lines (Pos.51-60) matching 80101-00's recorded count of 10; 79201-00 has 13 weld lines (Pos.81-93) matching 80201-00's recorded count of 13. Only counts were available for comparison (not the UWC fixture's full weld-type list), so this supports a same-design-different-scale hypothesis but doesn't confirm the weld types themselves match -- flagged for the cross-analysis chat, not resolved here.

New source anomaly, preserved as-is: 79201-00 Pos.46 (part 79201-42, Gewindeinsert) shows Werkstoff norm 'EN 10025-2' (a carbon-steel norm) paired with grade '1.4301' (an austenitic stainless grade), where the otherwise-identical Pos.40 (79201-36, same part type, same grade) correctly shows 'EN 10088-3'. Likely the same kind of copy-paste error already documented for 80101-11 in the UWC source (also an EN 10051/EN 10088-2 mismatch).

79102-00 is confirmed shared identically by both 79100-00 and 79200-00 within this product (already known to be shared cross-product with 80100-00/80200-00). Not re-extracted here -- its own 2 fabricated parts + 1 weld line already live in stadler_structured.json under parent_assembly '79102-00'.

No xlsx/Inventor export available for this source -- no part-photo thumbnails, no independent Item-hierarchy cross-check for 79000 SWC, unlike 80000 UWC which had both. See 'images' key above for the backfill approach.

Insulation sub-assemblies 79110-00 (3.64 kg) and 79210-00 (4.87 kg) were not provided -- masses known only via parent reference (79100-00, 79200-00 respectively), no part-level breakdown captured for this source. Consistent with the existing precedent that insulation sub-assemblies use a locally-sequential, non-stable numbering convention of low extraction value.

Mass-precision convention (parent assemblies reference child masses at higher decimal precision than the child's own rounded header total) holds at two more levels in this source: 79000-00 references 79100-00 at 96.16 kg (vs 79100-00's own rounded header of 96 kg) and 79200-00 at 172.59 kg (vs 173 kg); 79100-00 references 79101-00 at 89.45 kg (vs 89 kg). Reinforces this as a general document convention rather than a one-off.
